# Sequestrix

> Open-source Python tool for optimizing CO2 pipeline transport networks
> for carbon sequestration projects. Solves a Mixed-Integer Linear Program
> (MILP) to find minimum-cost source-sink pairings, pipeline routes, and
> build decisions. Published in SPE Journal (2026).

Sequestrix embeds existing pipelines into candidate network construction
and optimization - a capability not available in prior open-source tools
like SimCCS. It controls pipeline entry and egress using four tie-in
algorithms with exclusion zones, supports multiperiod planning with
time-varying capacities, and evaluates project economics including 45Q
tax credits. Built with Python, Streamlit, Gurobi/CPLEX, NetworkX,
and Plotly/Mapbox.

## Docs

- [Documentation site](https://www.sequestrix.com/): Full documentation with installation guide, input format, solver setup, case studies, mathematical formulation, and citation info
- [GitHub repository](https://github.com/davidpcg01/SEQUESTRIX): Source code, README, and issue tracker
- [Quick User Guide](https://github.com/davidpcg01/SEQUESTRIX/blob/main/SEQUESTRIX%20QUICK%20USER%20GUIDE.pdf): Step-by-step walkthrough with screenshots

## Research

- [SPE Journal Paper (2026)](https://doi.org/10.2118/214917-PA): Nnamdi, D. and Moghanloo, R. G. "Embedding Existing Pipelines in Design of CO2 Transportation Networks for Optimal Sequestration Economics." SPE Journal 31(01): 643-660. SPE-214917-PA.

## Key Capabilities

- Existing pipeline embedding with 4 tie-in/exclusion algorithms
- Delaunay triangulation for candidate network generation
- MILP optimization via Gurobi (primary) or CPLEX (fallback via PuLP)
- Multiperiod planning with time-varying capture, storage, and targets
- Scenario management: save, load, compare named optimization runs
- Streamlit web interface with Plotly/Mapbox interactive maps
- Results dashboard with economic classification and cost breakdowns
- Case studies: Enid-Purdy pipeline (Oklahoma), CarbonSAFE II (Osage County)
